items.q: If the discriminant of a quadratic is negative, what does it tell you about real roots?
items.correct: There are no real roots — the graph does not cross the x-axis.
items.misconception: There is one repeated root — the curve just touches the x-axis.
items.why: Students confuse 'discriminant zero' (one repeated root) with 'discriminant negative' (no real roots) because both feel like 'unusual' cases.

items.correct: The square root of x (the positive root when x is positive).
items.misconception: x^(1/2) means x divided by 2 — half of x.
items.random_wrong: x^(1/2) means 1 divided by x squared.
items.why: Fraction-as-division intuition takes over; students don't connect fractional exponents to roots.

items.q: What is the value of x^0 (for x not equal to zero)?
items.correct: 1 — any non-zero number to the power of zero equals 1.
items.misconception: x^0 equals 0 because raising something to the zero gives nothing.
items.random_wrong: x^0 equals x because the zero has no effect.
items.why: Students reason 'zero of anything is zero', applying additive intuition to exponents — they don't see x^0 as the result of x^1 / x^1 = 1.
The answer examiners want is: 0. “-4” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's No real roots. “Two real roots” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The real figure is 1. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
